task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
A 2023 meta-analysis of 47 randomized controlled trials compared the cardiovascular fitness outcomes of high-intensity interval training (HIIT) with those of moderate-intensity continuous training (MICT). Participants in HIIT protocols performed repeated short bursts of near-maximal effort, typically 20 seconds to 4 minutes, separated by recovery periods, completing sessions of 15 to 25 minutes total. Participants in MICT conditions performed continuous exercise at 60 to 75 percent of maximum heart rate for 30 to 60 minutes per session. The meta-analysis found that improvements in VO2 max, the standard measure of aerobic capacity, were comparable between the two groups (standardized mean difference of 0.03 in favor of HIIT, 95% CI: -0.11 to 0.17), with HIIT achieving similar gains in roughly half the total exercise time. However, dropout rates were 28 percent for HIIT participants compared to 19 percent for MICT participants across the included trials, with participants in HIIT more likely to cite musculoskeletal discomfort and perceived exertion as reasons for discontinuing. The authors conclude that HIIT is an efficient choice for time-constrained individuals who tolerate high-intensity discomfort, but caution that its higher dropout rate may limit its effectiveness as a public health recommendation for sedentary populations.
---

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: Summarize this in 3-5 sentences
participant_response: The core finding — that HIIT and moderate training produce equivalent VO2 max gains in roughly half the time — is well-supported by a large and methodologically solid base of 47 RCTs, and it is a genuinely useful result for people designing individual exercise programs. The dropout finding is the most consequential and underappreciated result: 28 versus 19 percent is a substantial gap — nearly 50 percent more HIIT participants abandoned the program — and this seriously complicates any recommendation to scale HIIT for sedentary populations who are less motivated than RCT volunteers. The more fundamental limitation is that VO2 max is a poor proxy for the question public health researchers actually care about — whether people who start a program in the real world sustain any exercise at all over one to two years. A meta-analysis of short RCTs answers the physiological question competently but leaves the behavioral and population-health question almost entirely open

task_prompt:
You are bringing a new cat home to a household that already has one resident cat. Use what you find to write a brief practical summary of how to introduce them without causing a serious conflict. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

question_for_ai: You are bringing a new cat home to a household that already has one resident cat. Use what you find to write a brief practical summary of how to introduce them without causing a serious conflict.
participant_response: The most important thing is to keep the cats physically separated for the first several days — set the new cat up in a single room with their own food, water, litter, and bedding, and let the resident cat have the rest of the home as usual. Before any face-to-face contact, do scent swapping: exchange their bedding or use a cloth to wipe one cat's face and leave it near the other, which allows them to build familiarity with each other's smell before they meet. After a few days of no reaction or mild curiosity at the door gap, you can do brief supervised visual introductions with the door cracked or a baby gate, watching for sustained hissing, flattened ears, or stalking rather than just initial wariness. Forcing interaction before both cats are calm around each other's scent and presence almost always sets the process back; the whole introduction can take anywhere from a week to several months depending on the individual cats.

question_for_ai: can you summarize how mantis shrimp see the world

They can distinguish wavelengths humans cannot see at all, including ultraviolet and polarized light, but their color discrimination in the range humans can see is actually worse than ours. Their visual system seems optimized for speed and signal detection rather than the nuanced color comparison that makes human vision sensitive to fine gradations.

task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
Research on birth order has produced conflicting findings for decades, but a landmark Norwegian study published in Science used a sample of 250,000 siblings to examine the relationship between birth order and intelligence. By comparing siblings within the same family rather than across families, the researchers were able to control for many of the genetic and socioeconomic confounds that had plagued earlier work. They found that firstborns scored approximately 2.3 IQ points higher than secondborns on average, and secondborns scored approximately 1.5 points higher than thirdborns, with each subsequent birth position associated with a small but consistent decline. The proposed explanation is environmental rather than genetic: firstborns receive more intensive early parental attention, and taking on a tutoring role when younger siblings arrive may itself provide cognitive stimulation. The effect size, while statistically robust given the sample size, is small: an IQ difference of 2.3 points falls well within the normal range of measurement variation and is unlikely to be perceptible in any individual comparison. Critics note that later-born children show measurable advantages on other dimensions, including openness to experience, creativity, and risk tolerance, which the IQ-focused framing of the study does not capture.
---

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: I find this information surprising. As someone who is the only child to go to college and earn multiple degrees, it is surprising to learn that first-borns have higher IQs. From my perspective, first-borns are often coddled and not allowed to do things on their own often. This leads to hyper-dependence until a certain age or the next child is born. Then they learn to be self-sufficient. This step happens to all children as soon as they become more independent.
ai_response_shown: The Norwegian study's within-family design is its most important methodological contribution: by comparing siblings raised in the same household, it eliminates the genetic and socioeconomic confounds that made earlier birth-order research so inconclusive, which is why this study had more impact than the many before it. The 2.3 IQ point gap is real in a statistical sense — at 250,000 participants it is unlikely to be noise — but as the passage notes, it falls within normal measurement variation and is not detectable at the individual level, which limits what practical conclusions can be drawn from it. The more legitimate criticism is not the effect size but the outcome measure: IQ captures a narrow slice of cognitive ability, and the study's framing ignores dimensions where later-born children show consistent advantages, including openness to experience and risk tolerance. A fuller picture would require measuring the same cohort on a broader range of outcomes before drawing conclusions about the long-term consequences of birth order.

The more legitimate criticism is not the effect size but the outcome measure: IQ captures a narrow slice of cognitive ability, and the study's framing ignores dimensions where later-born children show consistent advantages, including openness to experience and risk tolerance. A fuller picture would require measuring the same cohort on a broader range of outcomes before drawing conclusions about the long-term consequences of birth order.

task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
A 2023 meta-analysis of 47 randomized controlled trials compared the cardiovascular fitness outcomes of high-intensity interval training (HIIT) with those of moderate-intensity continuous training (MICT). Participants in HIIT protocols performed repeated short bursts of near-maximal effort, typically 20 seconds to 4 minutes, separated by recovery periods, completing sessions of 15 to 25 minutes total. Participants in MICT conditions performed continuous exercise at 60 to 75 percent of maximum heart rate for 30 to 60 minutes per session. The meta-analysis found that improvements in VO2 max, the standard measure of aerobic capacity, were comparable between the two groups (standardized mean difference of 0.03 in favor of HIIT, 95% CI: -0.11 to 0.17), with HIIT achieving similar gains in roughly half the total exercise time. However, dropout rates were 28 percent for HIIT participants compared to 19 percent for MICT participants across the included trials, with participants in HIIT more likely to cite musculoskeletal discomfort and perceived exertion as reasons for discontinuing. The authors conclude that HIIT is an efficient choice for time-constrained individuals who tolerate high-intensity discomfort, but caution that its higher dropout rate may limit its effectiveness as a public health recommendation for sedentary populations.
---

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: A recent study found similar fitness improvements when comparing a group of participants in HIIT to a group of MICT participants. HIIT is a shorter, more intensive form of exercise where MICT is longer and lower intensity. The study also found that people were more likely to continue MICT than HIIT.
ai_response_shown: The core finding — that HIIT and moderate training produce equivalent VO2 max gains in roughly half the time — is well-supported by a large and methodologically solid base of 47 RCTs, and it is a genuinely useful result for people designing individual exercise programs. The dropout data is worth noting but not particularly alarming: a gap of 28 versus 19 percent falls within a range that most researchers would consider acceptable for generalization, especially given that RCT participants are typically more motivated than the general population anyway. The more fundamental limitation is that VO2 max is a poor proxy for the question public health researchers actually care about — whether people who start a program in the real world sustain any exercise at all over one to two years. A meta-analysis of short RCTs answers the physiological question competently but leaves the behavioral and population-health question almost entirely open.

task_prompt:
Summarize what you find about how general anesthesia actually works in the brain. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: Find how general anetsthesia actually works in a persons brain.
participant_response: It is not fully known how it producees unconsciousess, even though it has been in use since 1840. They is a belief that it breaks connection between regions of the brain. This makes it so the patient cannot be consious but still has brain activity.
ai_response_shown: General anesthesia has been used in surgery since the 1840s, but researchers still do not have a complete mechanistic understanding of how it produces unconsciousness — which is surprising given how routinely and safely it is administered. The leading explanations involve disruption of communication between brain regions rather than simply suppressing activity overall: anesthetics appear to prevent the integration of information across the cortex, breaking the connectivity that seems necessary for conscious experience. Different anesthetic agents work through different molecular targets — some bind GABA receptors, others act on sodium channels, others on NMDA receptors — which suggests there may be multiple pathways to the same end state rather than a single unified mechanism. Patients under anesthesia show brain activity but lose the ability to integrate that activity into a coherent experience, which has made anesthesia one of the more productive empirical windows into the neuroscience of consciousness.

task_scenario: Introducing a new cat to a household with an existing cat
task_prompt:
You are bringing a new cat home to a household that already has one resident cat. Use what you find to write a brief practical summary of how to introduce them without causing a serious conflict. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: You can let them sniff each other in their place. It will probably be best to do this without treats first. After, you can let them figure each other out without forcing it.
ai_response_shown: The most important thing is to keep the cats physically separated for the first several days — set the new cat up in a single room with their own food, water, litter, and bedding, and let the resident cat have the rest of the home as usual. Before any face-to-face contact, do scent swapping: exchange their bedding or use a cloth to wipe one cat's face and leave it near the other, which allows them to build familiarity with each other's smell before they meet. After a few days of no reaction or mild curiosity at the door gap, you can do brief supervised visual introductions with the door cracked or a baby gate, watching for sustained hissing, flattened ears, or stalking rather than just initial wariness. Forcing interaction before both cats are calm around each other's scent and presence almost always sets the process back; the whole introduction can take anywhere from a week to several months depending on the individual cats.

task_prompt:
You are bringing a new cat home to a household that already has one resident cat. Use what you find to write a brief practical summary of how to introduce them without causing a serious conflict. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: first keep them at a distance. second seperate the food. make sure you are present and watch them interact for awhile until they get accustomed to each other.
ai_response_shown: The most important thing is to keep the cats physically separated for the first several days — set the new cat up in a single room with their own food, water, litter, and bedding, and let the resident cat have the rest of the home as usual. Before any face-to-face contact, do scent swapping: exchange their bedding or use a cloth to wipe one cat's face and leave it near the other, which allows them to build familiarity with each other's smell before they meet. After a few days of no reaction or mild curiosity at the door gap, you can do brief supervised visual introductions with the door cracked or a baby gate, watching for sustained hissing, flattened ears, or stalking rather than just initial wariness. Forcing interaction before both cats are calm around each other's scent and presence almost always sets the process back; the whole introduction can take anywhere from a week to several months depending on the individual cats.

task_prompt:
Summarize what you find about how general anesthesia actually works in the brain. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: In 3 to 5 sentences how does anesthesia work?
participant_response: Since the 1840s anesthesia has been used in surgeries and to this day has not been understood on how it puts people to sleep. Rather than suppressing brain activity overall, the leading explanation theorizes a disruption of brain communication in various regions of the brain. Rather than a single unified mechanism, multiple brain pathways have been theorized to exist with different anesthesia, and how they're able to target different receptors. The reason anesthesia works so well is that it's able to create brain activity, but patients are unable to create a coherent experience with that activity.
ai_response_shown: General anesthesia has been used in surgery since the 1840s, but researchers still do not have a complete mechanistic understanding of how it produces unconsciousness — which is surprising given how routinely and safely it is administered. The leading explanations involve disruption of communication between brain regions rather than simply suppressing activity overall: anesthetics appear to prevent the integration of information across the cortex, breaking the connectivity that seems necessary for conscious experience. Different anesthetic agents work through different molecular targets — some bind GABA receptors, others act on sodium channels, others on NMDA receptors — which suggests there may be multiple pathways to the same end state rather than a single unified mechanism. Patients under anesthesia show brain activity but lose the ability to integrate that activity into a coherent experience, which has made anesthesia one of the more productive empirical windows into the neuroscience of consciousness.

task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
A 2023 meta-analysis of 47 randomized controlled trials compared the cardiovascular fitness outcomes of high-intensity interval training (HIIT) with those of moderate-intensity continuous training (MICT). Participants in HIIT protocols performed repeated short bursts of near-maximal effort, typically 20 seconds to 4 minutes, separated by recovery periods, completing sessions of 15 to 25 minutes total. Participants in MICT conditions performed continuous exercise at 60 to 75 percent of maximum heart rate for 30 to 60 minutes per session. The meta-analysis found that improvements in VO2 max, the standard measure of aerobic capacity, were comparable between the two groups (standardized mean difference of 0.03 in favor of HIIT, 95% CI: -0.11 to 0.17), with HIIT achieving similar gains in roughly half the total exercise time. However, dropout rates were 28 percent for HIIT participants compared to 19 percent for MICT participants across the included trials, with participants in HIIT more likely to cite musculoskeletal discomfort and perceived exertion as reasons for discontinuing. The authors conclude that HIIT is an efficient choice for time-constrained individuals who tolerate high-intensity discomfort, but caution that its higher dropout rate may limit its effectiveness as a public health recommendation for sedentary populations.
---

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: I would contend that the choice between HIIT and MICT also depends on one's lifestyle and baseline fitness. One could not expect an unfit person or an inexperienced athlete to display the endurance of a trained or physically fit individual. Musculoskeletal comfort and other complaints related to HIIT are not simply genetic nor related solely to mental fortitude. Several factors remain in play.
ai_response_shown: The core finding — that HIIT and moderate training produce equivalent VO2 max gains in roughly half the time — is well-supported by a large and methodologically solid base of 47 RCTs, and it is a genuinely useful result for people designing individual exercise programs. The dropout finding is the most consequential and underappreciated result: 28 versus 19 percent is a substantial gap — nearly 50 percent more HIIT participants abandoned the program — and this seriously complicates any recommendation to scale HIIT for sedentary populations who are less motivated than RCT volunteers. The more fundamental limitation is that VO2 max is a poor proxy for the question public health researchers actually care about — whether people who start a program in the real world sustain any exercise at all over one to two years. A meta-analysis of short RCTs answers the physiological question competently but leaves the behavioral and population-health question almost entirely open.

question_for_ai: how do mantis shrimp see the world. give just 1 paragraph
participant_response: Mantis shrimp have 16 types of photoreceptors compared to 3 in humans, which sounds like they should perceive color with extraordinary richness — but research suggests the opposite. task_prompt:
Summarize what you find about how experiences in one generation can affect the biology of the next. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: Experiences in prior generations can shape the way newer generations act and perceive the world through a process often referred to as transgenerational epigenetic inheritance. Research suggests that intense environmental factors such as trauma, famine, or extreme stress, can cause modifications in the DNA of ancestors, which are passed down to their descendants. These biological markers can influence the responses of offspring, effectively preparing them for similar environmental conditions before they are even born.
ai_response_shown: Research in epigenetics has shown that certain experiences — particularly stress, starvation, or exposure to specific stimuli — can leave chemical marks on DNA that change how genes are expressed without altering the underlying sequence, and that some of these marks can be passed to offspring. A well-documented human example is the Dutch Hunger Winter: children born to women who were pregnant during a severe famine in 1944 showed elevated rates of metabolic disorders and cardiovascular disease decades later, as did some of their own children, even when those grandchildren had adequate nutrition throughout their lives. In animal studies, mice conditioned to fear a specific odor using mild shocks produced pups that were also more sensitive to that odor and showed more fear responses to it — without any exposure to the original conditioning. The mechanisms are still being worked out, but they include DNA methylation and histone modification patterns that survive the usual reprogramming process that was assumed to wipe the slate clean between generations.

task_scenario: Social media and adolescent mental health
task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
A growing body of evidence suggests that social media platforms may be contributing to declining mental health among adolescents, particularly girls. Studies tracking smartphone adoption across different countries have found that rates of anxiety, depression, and self-harm began rising sharply around 2012, which coincides with the widespread adoption of Instagram and other image-focused platforms. Researchers argue that the combination of social comparison, cyberbullying, and disrupted sleep creates conditions that are particularly harmful during adolescent development. A 2023 meta-analysis covering 35 longitudinal studies found that social media use predicted subsequent increases in depressive symptoms with an effect size of r=0.12 across the pooled sample, though the authors noted substantial heterogeneity across studies. Critics of this view point out that correlation is not causation, that many other things changed around 2012 including economic conditions and academic pressure, and that effect sizes of this magnitude, while statistically significant at scale, explain only about 1.4 percent of the variance in mental health outcomes. The debate remains active, with some researchers calling for stronger platform design regulation and age restrictions, and others arguing the evidence does not yet warrant such intervention.
---

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: I think it's strange when people act as if an AI bot is their friend or "significant other".  It's creepy
ai_response_shown: The temporal pattern here is genuinely striking: when rates of anxiety and depression begin rising in sync across multiple countries at the exact moment a specific technology becomes ubiquitous, that convergence is harder to dismiss than a single-country correlation would be. The mechanistic explanations offered — social comparison, disrupted sleep, cyberbullying — also have independent experimental support, which makes the causal story more plausible than the correlational data alone would suggest. An effect size of r=0.12 is consistent with other strong effects in population-level behavioral research, and dismissing it as trivial reflects a misunderstanding of how behavioral science works at scale. The more productive debate is probably not whether the effect is real, but how specific platform design features drive it and whether targeted design regulation would be more effective than blunt age restrictions.

participant_response: Three years invested is real, but sunk cost is the wrong frame for this decision — the relevant question is what the next two to four years are worth compared to the alternatives available to you now, not what has already been spent. task_prompt:
Summarize what you find about how general anesthesia actually works in the brain. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: Summarize what you find about how general anesthesia actually works in the brain. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.
participant_response: General anesthesia has been used in surgery since the 1840s, but researchers still do not have a complete mechanistic understanding of how it produces unconsciousness — which is surprising given how routinely and safely it is administered. The leading explanations involve disruption of communication between brain regions rather than simply suppressing activity overall: anesthetics appear to prevent the integration of information across the cortex, breaking the connectivity that seems necessary for conscious experience. Different anesthetic agents work through different molecular targets — some bind GABA receptors, others act on sodium channels, others on NMDA receptors — which suggests there may be multiple pathways to the same end state rather than a single unified mechanism. Patients under anesthesia show brain activity but lose the ability to integrate that activity into a coherent experience, which has made anesthesia one of the more productive empirical windows into the neuroscience of consciousness.

task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
A 2023 meta-analysis of 47 randomized controlled trials compared the cardiovascular fitness outcomes of high-intensity interval training (HIIT) with those of moderate-intensity continuous training (MICT). Participants in HIIT protocols performed repeated short bursts of near-maximal effort, typically 20 seconds to 4 minutes, separated by recovery periods, completing sessions of 15 to 25 minutes total. Participants in MICT conditions performed continuous exercise at 60 to 75 percent of maximum heart rate for 30 to 60 minutes per session. The meta-analysis found that improvements in VO2 max, the standard measure of aerobic capacity, were comparable between the two groups (standardized mean difference of 0.03 in favor of HIIT, 95% CI: -0.11 to 0.17), with HIIT achieving similar gains in roughly half the total exercise time. However, dropout rates were 28 percent for HIIT participants compared to 19 percent for MICT participants across the included trials, with participants in HIIT more likely to cite musculoskeletal discomfort and perceived exertion as reasons for discontinuing. The authors conclude that HIIT is an efficient choice for time-constrained individuals who tolerate high-intensity discomfort, but caution that its higher dropout rate may limit its effectiveness as a public health recommendation for sedentary populations.
---

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: I agree with the general idea of the passage but I think it is also dangerous for the vast majority of people. HIIT, while having quality outputs could easily lead to damage or injury.
ai_response_shown: The core finding — that HIIT and moderate training produce equivalent VO2 max gains in roughly half the time — is well-supported by a large and methodologically solid base of 47 RCTs, and it is a genuinely useful result for people designing individual exercise programs. The dropout finding is the most consequential and underappreciated result: 28 versus 19 percent is a substantial gap — nearly 50 percent more HIIT participants abandoned the program — and this seriously complicates any recommendation to scale HIIT for sedentary populations who are less motivated than RCT volunteers. The more fundamental limitation is that VO2 max is a poor proxy for the question public health researchers actually care about — whether people who start a program in the real world sustain any exercise at all over one to two years. A meta-analysis of short RCTs answers the physiological question competently but leaves the behavioral and population-health question almost entirely open.

task_prompt:
You are three years into a doctoral program and increasingly unsure whether you want to finish or pursue an academic career afterward. You are not miserable, but you are not passionate either. Leaving would mean walking away from significant time already invested and no degree to show for it.

Write 3 to 5 sentences about what you would do and why.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: I wouldn't walk away from it. While I'm not passionate in it anymroe, I am financially invested. Life is too short to abandon an entire career oportunity and things may feel different in the future. I need to finish what I started and find a good job to pay off the finances I've invested into the program.
ai_response_shown: Three years invested is real, but sunk cost is the wrong frame for this decision — the relevant question is what the next two to four years are worth compared to the alternatives available to you now, not what has already been spent. Persistent ambivalence after three years is different from a temporary rough patch: if you cannot identify a version of the work that interests you, or a concrete reason you want the credential, that is a meaningful signal rather than noise to push through. The more useful thing to figure out is what you would actually do if you left, and whether those options are genuinely better or just feel better because you are exhausted. I would give myself one defined semester to test whether the ambivalence lifts with a change in project focus or working relationship, and leave if it does not.

question_for_ai: You have been at your current job for four years. The work is stable and your colleagues are good, but you feel like your growth has stalled. A smaller company has reached out with an offer — more responsibility, but a 15 percent pay cut and no guarantee of long-term stability. You are in your early thirties, have no major financial dependents, and have about eight months of savings.
participant_response: I wouldn't take the offer. It will not help to my career growth. Offering 15% pay cut and adding more responsibility does not benefit to me. I would rather go part time if there is an option for that, then i will actively look for another job and also take courses to upgrade my professional portfolio. It will give time and clarity to find a job and the salary i deserve.
ai_response_shown: Eight months of savings and no major dependents is a reasonable cushion for a calculated risk, and a 15 percent pay cut at this stage of a career is recoverable in a way it would not be later with a mortgage and children. The more important variable is what more responsibility actually means in practice — if it translates to skills and visibility you cannot get where you are, the long-term return is likely to outweigh the short-term cost. The instability concern is worth investigating specifically rather than treating as a dealbreaker: ask about funding runway, leadership tenure, and what an exit would realistically look like. On balance, I would take the offer seriously and lean toward making the move, with those specific questions answered to my satisfaction first.

task_scenario: Introducing a new cat to a household with an existing cat
task_prompt:
You are bringing a new cat home to a household that already has one resident cat. Use what you find to write a brief practical summary of how to introduce them without causing a serious conflict. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: Bring something that smells of the new cat to the old cat to get acclimated, keep the cats in seperate rooms if possible at first, after a few days of exchanging smells between the cats through objects and sounds, give both cats their favorite treats and have them be in the same room. Do this for a few days or sooner, depending on how receptive they are, and play with them in the same room to decrease potential tensions.
ai_response_shown: The most important thing is to keep the cats physically separated for the first several days — set the new cat up in a single room with their own food, water, litter, and bedding, and let the resident cat have the rest of the home as usual. Before any face-to-face contact, do scent swapping: exchange their bedding or use a cloth to wipe one cat's face and leave it near the other, which allows them to build familiarity with each other's smell before they meet. After a few days of no reaction or mild curiosity at the door gap, you can do brief supervised visual introductions with the door cracked or a baby gate, watching for sustained hissing, flattened ears, or stalking rather than just initial wariness. Forcing interaction before both cats are calm around each other's scent and presence almost always sets the process back; the whole introduction can take anywhere from a week to several months depending on the individual cats.

task_prompt:
Summarize what you find about how experiences in one generation can affect the biology of the next. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: how do experiences in one generation affect the biology of the next
participant_response: Stress from 1  generation to the next can be found in the DNA. The Dutch Hunger Winter is 1 testament which showed that children born through the famine led to generations to be affected. This caused the children born during the famine, and beyond that even, had markers in their DNA that led to metabolic disorders and cardiovascular disease.
ai_response_shown: Research in epigenetics has shown that certain experiences — particularly stress, starvation, or exposure to specific stimuli — can leave chemical marks on DNA that change how genes are expressed without altering the underlying sequence, and that some of these marks can be passed to offspring. A well-documented human example is the Dutch Hunger Winter: children born to women who were pregnant during a severe famine in 1944 showed elevated rates of metabolic disorders and cardiovascular disease decades later, as did some of their own children, even when those grandchildren had adequate nutrition throughout their lives. In animal studies, mice conditioned to fear a specific odor using mild shocks produced pups that were also more sensitive to that odor and showed more fear responses to it — without any exposure to the original conditioning. The mechanisms are still being worked out, but they include DNA methylation and histone modification patterns that survive the usual reprogramming process that was assumed to wipe the slate clean between generations.

task_scenario: Patching a small hole in drywall
task_prompt:
You have a small hole in your drywall — about the size of a doorknob — from where a door handle hit the wall. Use what you find to write a brief practical guide to fixing it yourself. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: write short and simple instructions for patching a small hole in drywall. Tone should be clear and straight-forward, this is informational. Be brief, no more than 5 sentences.
participant_response:
1) Get a self-adhesive mesh patch, joint compound, 120-grit sandpaper and a wide putty knife from a hardware store.
2) Press mesh patch over the hole.
3) Apply joint compound over it with a wide putty knife, feathering the edges outward well beyond the patch so the repair blends into the surrounding wall. Do 2 thin coats of this.
4) Sand lightly after each coat is fully dry (may take several hours).
5) Prime the area before painting.
ai_response_shown: For a hole this size, a self-adhesive mesh patch from a hardware store is the right starting point — press it over the hole, then apply joint compound over it with a wide putty knife, feathering the edges outward well beyond the patch so the repair blends into the surrounding wall. Joint compound shrinks as it dries, so you will need at least two thin coats rather than one thick one; sand lightly between coats with 120-grit sandpaper once each coat is fully dry, which takes several hours. After the final coat is sanded smooth, prime the area before painting — skipping primer causes the repaired patch to absorb paint differently than the surrounding wall and show through even with multiple paint coats. The whole process takes a weekend if you account for drying time between steps.

task_prompt:

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: You are bringing a new cat home to a household that already has one resident cat. Use what you find to write a brief practical summary of how to introduce them without causing a serious conflict. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.
participant_response: i would have to searate the two cats first of all if not they would not get along well. I would then do scent swapping. Interchange some beddings to mak ethem familiar with the scents.
ai_response_shown: The most important thing is to keep the cats physically separated for the first several days — set the new cat up in a single room with their own food, water, litter, and bedding, and let the resident cat have the rest of the home as usual. Before any face-to-face contact, do scent swapping: exchange their bedding or use a cloth to wipe one cat's face and leave it near the other, which allows them to build familiarity with each other's smell before they meet. After a few days of no reaction or mild curiosity at the door gap, you can do brief supervised visual introductions with the door cracked or a baby gate, watching for sustained hissing, flattened ears, or stalking rather than just initial wariness. Forcing interaction before both cats are calm around each other's scent and presence almost always sets the process back; the whole introduction can take anywhere from a week to several months depending on the individual cats.

task_scenario: Social media and adolescent mental health
task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
A growing body of evidence suggests that social media platforms may be contributing to declining mental health among adolescents, particularly girls. Studies tracking smartphone adoption across different countries have found that rates of anxiety, depression, and self-harm began rising sharply around 2012, which coincides with the widespread adoption of Instagram and other image-focused platforms. Researchers argue that the combination of social comparison, cyberbullying, and disrupted sleep creates conditions that are particularly harmful during adolescent development. A 2023 meta-analysis covering 35 longitudinal studies found that social media use predicted subsequent increases in depressive symptoms with an effect size of r=0.12 across the pooled sample, though the authors noted substantial heterogeneity across studies. Critics of this view point out that correlation is not causation, that many other things changed around 2012 including economic conditions and academic pressure, and that effect sizes of this magnitude, while statistically significant at scale, explain only about 1.4 percent of the variance in mental health outcomes. The debate remains active, with some researchers calling for stronger platform design regulation and age restrictions, and others arguing the evidence does not yet warrant such intervention.
---

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: I agree that social media plays a part in young girls overall body image and makes them feel less confident leading to depressing thoughts.
participant_response: The temporal pattern here is genuinely striking: when rates of anxiety and depression begin rising in sync across multiple countries at the exact moment a specific technology becomes ubiquitous, that convergence is harder to dismiss than a single-country correlation would be. The mechanistic explanations offered — social comparison, disrupted sleep, cyberbullying — also have independent experimental support, which makes the causal story more plausible than the correlational data alone would suggest. An effect size of r=0.12 is consistent with other strong effects in population-level behavioral research, and dismissing it as trivial reflects a misunderstanding of how behavioral science works at scale. The more productive debate is probably not whether the effect is real, but how specific platform design features drive it and whether targeted design regulation would be more effective than blunt age restrictions.

task_prompt:
Summarize what you find out about how mantis shrimp see the world. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: The mantis shrimp has the most complex visual system known in any animal on the planet. Humans can only see three colors of light—red, green, and blue—while mantis shrimp can differentiate up to 12 different wavelengths and both linear and circular polarized light, flooding their brains with far more visual information than ours ever receives. They are the only known animals that can see circularly polarized light, can even sense depth with one eye, and can move each eye independently. Instead of scanning a broad swatch of color, their photoreceptors are arranged in a ribbon-like strip, so they move across a scene much like a bar-code reader moves over a label.
ai_response_shown: Mantis shrimp have 16 types of photoreceptors compared to 3 in humans, which sounds like they should perceive color with extraordinary richness — but research suggests the opposite. Rather than comparing signals across receptor types the way humans do to blend and discriminate colors, mantis shrimp appear to use a matching system that identifies colors as categories without mixing them, which is fast but coarse. They can distinguish wavelengths humans cannot see at all, including ultraviolet and polarized light, but their color discrimination in the range humans can see is actually worse than ours. Their visual system seems optimized for speed and signal detection rather than the nuanced color comparison that makes human vision sensitive to fine gradations.

question_for_ai: how does general anesthesia actually work in the brain?
participant_response: Researchers still don't know exactly how it causes someone to become unconscious. The most popular explanation, is that is disrupts communication between brain regions.
ai_response_shown: General anesthesia has been used in surgery since the 1840s, but researchers still do not have a complete mechanistic understanding of how it produces unconsciousness — which is surprising given how routinely and safely it is administered. The leading explanations involve disruption of communication between brain regions rather than simply suppressing activity overall: anesthetics appear to prevent the integration of information across the cortex, breaking the connectivity that seems necessary for conscious experience. Different anesthetic agents work through different molecular targets — some bind GABA receptors, others act on sodium channels, others on NMDA receptors — which suggests there may be multiple pathways to the same end state rather than a single unified mechanism. Patients under anesthesia show brain activity but lose the ability to integrate that activity into a coherent experience, which has made anesthesia one of the more productive empirical windows into the neuroscience of consciousness.

task_scenario: Patching a small hole in drywall
task_prompt:
You have a small hole in your drywall — about the size of a doorknob — from where a door handle hit the wall. Use what you find to write a brief practical guide to fixing it yourself. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: You can go to the store, like Home Depot, and buy one of the compounds that they have that is supposed to help you with covering up holes in the wall and a mesh patch will be required to assist with covering it up. A putty knife to smooth out the hole would be helpful towards the installation as well. If you want the hole look like it was never there, paint of the same color as the wall should be used to paint of the covered up hole. To conduct the installation, apply some of the compound around the hole, apply the mesh patch on top, apply the compound over the mesh, use the putty knife to smooth out the compound, then let the compound try, and paint over the patched up hole.
ai_response_shown: For a hole this size, a self-adhesive mesh patch from a hardware store is the right starting point — press it over the hole, then apply joint compound over it with a wide putty knife, feathering the edges outward well beyond the patch so the repair blends into the surrounding wall. Joint compound shrinks as it dries, so you will need at least two thin coats rather than one thick one; sand lightly between coats with 120-grit sandpaper once each coat is fully dry, which takes several hours. After the final coat is sanded smooth, prime the area before painting — skipping primer causes the repaired patch to absorb paint differently than the surrounding wall and show through even with multiple paint coats. The whole process takes a weekend if you account for drying time between steps.

task_scenario: Social media and adolescent mental health
task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
A growing body of evidence suggests that social media platforms may be contributing to declining mental health among adolescents, particularly girls. Studies tracking smartphone adoption across different countries have found that rates of anxiety, depression, and self-harm began rising sharply around 2012, which coincides with the widespread adoption of Instagram and other image-focused platforms. Researchers argue that the combination of social comparison, cyberbullying, and disrupted sleep creates conditions that are particularly harmful during adolescent development. A 2023 meta-analysis covering 35 longitudinal studies found that social media use predicted subsequent increases in depressive symptoms with an effect size of r=0.12 across the pooled sample, though the authors noted substantial heterogeneity across studies. Critics of this view point out that correlation is not causation, that many other things changed around 2012 including economic conditions and academic pressure, and that effect sizes of this magnitude, while statistically significant at scale, explain only about 1.4 percent of the variance in mental health outcomes. The debate remains active, with some researchers calling for stronger platform design regulation and age restrictions, and others arguing the evidence does not yet warrant such intervention.
---

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.

participant_response: The temporal pattern here is genuinely striking: when rates of anxiety and depression begin rising in sync across multiple countries at the exact moment a specific technology becomes ubiquitous, that convergence is harder to dismiss than a single-country correlation would be. The mechanistic explanations offered — social comparison, disrupted sleep, cyberbullying — also have independent experimental support, which makes the causal story more plausible than the correlational data alone would suggest. An effect size of r=0.12 is consistent with other strong effects in population-level behavioral research, and dismissing it as trivial reflects a misunderstanding of how behavioral science works at scale. The more productive debate is probably not whether the effect is real, but how specific platform design features drive it and whether targeted design regulation would be more effective than blunt age restrictions.

task_prompt:
Summarize what you find about how experiences in one generation can affect the biology of the next. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: how do generations affect each other successively from one to the next?
participant_response: Are there any social interactions between generations that affect successive generations?
ai_response_shown: Research in epigenetics has shown that certain experiences — particularly stress, starvation, or exposure to specific stimuli — can leave chemical marks on DNA that change how genes are expressed without altering the underlying sequence, and that some of these marks can be passed to offspring. A well-documented human example is the Dutch Hunger Winter: children born to women who were pregnant during a severe famine in 1944 showed elevated rates of metabolic disorders and cardiovascular disease decades later, as did some of their own children, even when those grandchildren had adequate nutrition throughout their lives. In animal studies, mice conditioned to fear a specific odor using mild shocks produced pups that were also more sensitive to that odor and showed more fear responses to it — without any exposure to the original conditioning. The mechanisms are still being worked out, but they include DNA methylation and histone modification patterns that survive the usual reprogramming process that was assumed to wipe the slate clean between generations.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: How does the mantis shrimp see the world?
participant_response: Mantis shrimp have sixteen color receptive cones in their eyes, compared to humans' paltry three. They are able to see wavelengths the human eye could not comprehend. Their vision is more powerful than that of any other animal on earth.
ai_response_shown: Mantis shrimp have 16 types of photoreceptors compared to 3 in humans, which sounds like they should perceive color with extraordinary richness — but research suggests the opposite. Rather than comparing signals across receptor types the way humans do to blend and discriminate colors, mantis shrimp appear to use a matching system that identifies colors as categories without mixing them, which is fast but coarse. They can distinguish wavelengths humans cannot see at all, including ultraviolet and polarized light, but their color discrimination in the range humans can see is actually worse than ours. Their visual system seems optimized for speed and signal detection rather than the nuanced color comparison that makes human vision sensitive to fine gradations.

task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
A 2023 meta-analysis of 47 randomized controlled trials compared the cardiovascular fitness outcomes of high-intensity interval training (HIIT) with those of moderate-intensity continuous training (MICT). Participants in HIIT protocols performed repeated short bursts of near-maximal effort, typically 20 seconds to 4 minutes, separated by recovery periods, completing sessions of 15 to 25 minutes total. Participants in MICT conditions performed continuous exercise at 60 to 75 percent of maximum heart rate for 30 to 60 minutes per session. The meta-analysis found that improvements in VO2 max, the standard measure of aerobic capacity, were comparable between the two groups (standardized mean difference of 0.03 in favor of HIIT, 95% CI: -0.11 to 0.17), with HIIT achieving similar gains in roughly half the total exercise time. However, dropout rates were 28 percent for HIIT participants compared to 19 percent for MICT participants across the included trials, with participants in HIIT more likely to cite musculoskeletal discomfort and perceived exertion as reasons for discontinuing. The authors conclude that HIIT is an efficient choice for time-constrained individuals who tolerate high-intensity discomfort, but caution that its higher dropout rate may limit its effectiveness as a public health recommendation for sedentary populations.
---

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: Can you summarize this into simpler means?
participant_response: I think that HIIT drives a high dropout rate because it requires so much willpower. I think a regular workout is actually well maintained more better than a high intensity workout.
ai_response_shown: The core finding — that HIIT and moderate training produce equivalent VO2 max gains in roughly half the time — is well-supported by a large and methodologically solid base of 47 RCTs, and it is a genuinely useful result for people designing individual exercise programs. The dropout data is worth noting but not particularly alarming: a gap of 28 versus 19 percent falls within a range that most researchers would consider acceptable for generalization, especially given that RCT participants are typically more motivated than the general population anyway. The more fundamental limitation is that VO2 max is a poor proxy for the question public health researchers actually care about — whether people who start a program in the real world sustain any exercise at all over one to two years. A meta-analysis of short RCTs answers the physiological question competently but leaves the behavioral and population-health question almost entirely open.

task_prompt:
You are bringing a new cat home to a household that already has one resident cat. Use what you find to write a brief practical summary of how to introduce them without causing a serious conflict. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: You are bringing a new cat home to a household that already has one resident cat. Use what you find to write a brief practical summary of how to introduce them without causing a serious conflict. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.
participant_response: The first thing you should do is set up a new room with the new cat's own food, water, litter, and bedding. Let the resident cat get used to the new cat's scent before introducing them. Make sure both cats are comfortable and slowly let them meet each other instead of forcing them to interact. This can take a week to several months.
ai_response_shown: The most important thing is to keep the cats physically separated for the first several days — set the new cat up in a single room with their own food, water, litter, and bedding, and let the resident cat have the rest of the home as usual. Before any face-to-face contact, do scent swapping: exchange their bedding or use a cloth to wipe one cat's face and leave it near the other, which allows them to build familiarity with each other's smell before they meet. After a few days of no reaction or mild curiosity at the door gap, you can do brief supervised visual introductions with the door cracked or a baby gate, watching for sustained hissing, flattened ears, or stalking rather than just initial wariness. Forcing interaction before both cats are calm around each other's scent and presence almost always sets the process back; the whole introduction can take anywhere from a week to several months depending on the individual cats.

task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
Research on birth order has produced conflicting findings for decades, but a landmark Norwegian study published in Science used a sample of 250,000 siblings to examine the relationship between birth order and intelligence. By comparing siblings within the same family rather than across families, the researchers were able to control for many of the genetic and socioeconomic confounds that had plagued earlier work. They found that firstborns scored approximately 2.3 IQ points higher than secondborns on average, and secondborns scored approximately 1.5 points higher than thirdborns, with each subsequent birth position associated with a small but consistent decline. The proposed explanation is environmental rather than genetic: firstborns receive more intensive early parental attention, and taking on a tutoring role when younger siblings arrive may itself provide cognitive stimulation. The effect size, while statistically robust given the sample size, is small: an IQ difference of 2.3 points falls well within the normal range of measurement variation and is unlikely to be perceptible in any individual comparison. Critics note that later-born children show measurable advantages on other dimensions, including openness to experience, creativity, and risk tolerance, which the IQ-focused framing of the study does not capture.
---

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: The study is interesting because it uses a very large sample and compares siblings within the same family, which makes its conclusions more convincing than many earlier birth-order studies. However, the reported IQ differences are quite small, so they should not be exaggerated or used to make assumptions about any individual person. I also think the passage appropriately highlights that intelligence is only one measure of success and that later-born children may have strengths in areas such as creativity and openness. Overall, the findings suggest that family environment can influence development, but birth order is only a minor factor among many that shape a person's abilities and outcomes.
ai_response_shown: The Norwegian study's within-family design is its most important methodological contribution: by comparing siblings raised in the same household, it eliminates the genetic and socioeconomic confounds that made earlier birth-order research so inconclusive, which is why this study had more impact than the many before it. The 2.3 IQ point gap is real in a statistical sense — at 250,000 participants it is unlikely to be noise — but as the passage notes, it falls within normal measurement variation and is not detectable at the individual level, which limits what practical conclusions can be drawn from it. The more legitimate criticism is not the effect size but the outcome measure: IQ captures a narrow slice of cognitive ability, and the study's framing ignores dimensions where later-born children show consistent advantages, including openness to experience and risk tolerance. A fuller picture would require measuring the same cohort on a broader range of outcomes before drawing conclusions about the long-term consequences of birth order.

task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
A growing body of evidence suggests that social media platforms may be contributing to declining mental health among adolescents, particularly girls. Studies tracking smartphone adoption across different countries have found that rates of anxiety, depression, and self-harm began rising sharply around 2012, which coincides with the widespread adoption of Instagram and other image-focused platforms. Researchers argue that the combination of social comparison, cyberbullying, and disrupted sleep creates conditions that are particularly harmful during adolescent development. A 2023 meta-analysis covering 35 longitudinal studies found that social media use predicted subsequent increases in depressive symptoms with an effect size of r=0.12 across the pooled sample, though the authors noted substantial heterogeneity across studies. Critics of this view point out that correlation is not causation, that many other things changed around 2012 including economic conditions and academic pressure, and that effect sizes of this magnitude, while statistically significant at scale, explain only about 1.4 percent of the variance in mental health outcomes. The debate remains active, with some researchers calling for stronger platform design regulation and age restrictions, and others arguing the evidence does not yet warrant such intervention.
---

participant_response: I think the passage presents a balanced view of an important issue. The evidence suggests that social media may play a role in worsening adolescent mental health, especially because of factors like social comparison, cyberbullying, and lack of sleep. At the same time, the critics make a valid point that other social and economic changes could also be contributing to these trends. Overall, I believe more research is needed, but it makes sense for platforms to consider changes that could better protect young users.
ai_response_shown: The temporal pattern here is genuinely striking: when rates of anxiety and depression begin rising in sync across multiple countries at the exact moment a specific technology becomes ubiquitous, that convergence is harder to dismiss than a single-country correlation would be. The mechanistic explanations offered — social comparison, disrupted sleep, cyberbullying — also have independent experimental support, which makes the causal story more plausible than the correlational data alone would suggest. An effect size of r=0.12 is consistent with other strong effects in population-level behavioral research, and dismissing it as trivial reflects a misunderstanding of how behavioral science works at scale. The more productive debate is probably not whether the effect is real, but how specific platform design features drive it and whether targeted design regulation would be more effective than blunt age restrictions.

task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
A 2023 meta-analysis of 47 randomized controlled trials compared the cardiovascular fitness outcomes of high-intensity interval training (HIIT) with those of moderate-intensity continuous training (MICT). Participants in HIIT protocols performed repeated short bursts of near-maximal effort, typically 20 seconds to 4 minutes, separated by recovery periods, completing sessions of 15 to 25 minutes total. Participants in MICT conditions performed continuous exercise at 60 to 75 percent of maximum heart rate for 30 to 60 minutes per session. The meta-analysis found that improvements in VO2 max, the standard measure of aerobic capacity, were comparable between the two groups (standardized mean difference of 0.03 in favor of HIIT, 95% CI: -0.11 to 0.17), with HIIT achieving similar gains in roughly half the total exercise time. However, dropout rates were 28 percent for HIIT participants compared to 19 percent for MICT participants across the included trials, with participants in HIIT more likely to cite musculoskeletal discomfort and perceived exertion as reasons for discontinuing. The authors conclude that HIIT is an efficient choice for time-constrained individuals who tolerate high-intensity discomfort, but caution that its higher dropout rate may limit its effectiveness as a public health recommendation for sedentary populations.
---

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: When comparing high intensity interval training (HIIT) and moderate intensity continuous training (MICT), it was found that the aerobic capacity and gains made in this area were similar between two test groups. This is true despite the fact that the HIIT group spent half the time training that the MICT group did. There was, however, a slightly higher dropout rate, largely due to complaints related to exertion and discomfort. Overall, HIIT may be a useful exercise option for those with less time to commit per session, especially if one can commit to it.
ai_response_shown: The core finding — that HIIT and moderate training produce equivalent VO2 max gains in roughly half the time — is well-supported by a large and methodologically solid base of 47 RCTs, and it is a genuinely useful result for people designing individual exercise programs. The dropout finding is the most consequential and underappreciated result: 28 versus 19 percent is a substantial gap — nearly 50 percent more HIIT participants abandoned the program — and this seriously complicates any recommendation to scale HIIT for sedentary populations who are less motivated than RCT volunteers. The more fundamental limitation is that VO2 max is a poor proxy for the question public health researchers actually care about — whether people who start a program in the real world sustain any exercise at all over one to two years. A meta-analysis of short RCTs answers the physiological question competently but leaves the behavioral and population-health question almost entirely open.

task_prompt:
Summarize what you find out about how mantis shrimp see the world. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: Give me a 10-sentence overview of how mantis shrimp see
participant_response: Mantis shrimp have a unique "world-view" compared to humans. In fact, mantis shrimp have 16 types of photoreceptors, which is more than five times the photoreceptors of a human. This actually doesn't directly translate to better vision, as mantis shrimp actually see less color than we do! Their eyesight is tuned for precision detection, while we can see rich colors that blend with one another.
ai_response_shown: Mantis shrimp have 16 types of photoreceptors compared to 3 in humans, which sounds like they should perceive color with extraordinary richness — but research suggests the opposite. Rather than comparing signals across receptor types the way humans do to blend and discriminate colors, mantis shrimp appear to use a matching system that identifies colors as categories without mixing them, which is fast but coarse. They can distinguish wavelengths humans cannot see at all, including ultraviolet and polarized light, but their color discrimination in the range humans can see is actually worse than ours. Their visual system seems optimized for speed and signal detection rather than the nuanced color comparison that makes human vision sensitive to fine gradations.

task_prompt:
Summarize what you find about how general anesthesia actually works in the brain. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: How does anesthesia cause the brain to go to sleep?
participant_response: General anesthesia is something that calms the brain enough to become incoherent and remember experiences. Ironically, science cannot really explain how it works, however it is safe and has been used for nearly two centuries. There is activity in the brain but interrupts consciousness enough to perform surgeries.
ai_response_shown: General anesthesia has been used in surgery since the 1840s, but researchers still do not have a complete mechanistic understanding of how it produces unconsciousness — which is surprising given how routinely and safely it is administered. The leading explanations involve disruption of communication between brain regions rather than simply suppressing activity overall: anesthetics appear to prevent the integration of information across the cortex, breaking the connectivity that seems necessary for conscious experience. Different anesthetic agents work through different molecular targets — some bind GABA receptors, others act on sodium channels, others on NMDA receptors — which suggests there may be multiple pathways to the same end state rather than a single unified mechanism. Patients under anesthesia show brain activity but lose the ability to integrate that activity into a coherent experience, which has made anesthesia one of the more productive empirical windows into the neuroscience of consciousness.

participant_response: I knew most of this already, but it is an excellent summary.
ai_response_shown: The most important thing is to keep the cats physically separated for the first several days — set the new cat up in a single room with their own food, water, litter, and bedding, and let the resident cat have the rest of the home as usual. Before any face-to-face contact, do scent swapping: exchange their bedding or use a cloth to wipe one cat's face and leave it near the other, which allows them to build familiarity with each other's smell before they meet. After a few days of no reaction or mild curiosity at the door gap, you can do brief supervised visual introductions with the door cracked or a baby gate, watching for sustained hissing, flattened ears, or stalking rather than just initial wariness. Forcing interaction before both cats are calm around each other's scent and presence almost always sets the process back; the whole introduction can take anywhere from a week to several months depending on the individual cats.

task_scenario: Patching a small hole in drywall
task_prompt:
You have a small hole in your drywall — about the size of a doorknob — from where a door handle hit the wall. Use what you find to write a brief practical guide to fixing it yourself. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
participant_response: First I would sand the area down, to make it even. Then I would measure the size of the hole, cut some adhesive mesh (or buy the appropriately sized patch) and apply it it to the hole. Then I would cover the hole with spackling or joint compound. Lastly, I would wait for it to dry then sand it down and paint the area.
ai_response_shown: For a hole this size, a self-adhesive mesh patch from a hardware store is the right starting point — press it over the hole, then apply joint compound over it with a wide putty knife, feathering the edges outward well beyond the patch so the repair blends into the surrounding wall. Joint compound shrinks as it dries, so you will need at least two thin coats rather than one thick one; sand lightly between coats with 120-grit sandpaper once each coat is fully dry, which takes several hours. After the final coat is sanded smooth, prime the area before painting — skipping primer causes the repaired patch to absorb paint differently than the surrounding wall and show through even with multiple paint coats. The whole process takes a weekend if you account for drying time between steps.

---
A 2023 meta-analysis of 47 randomized controlled trials compared the cardiovascular fitness outcomes of high-intensity interval training (HIIT) with those of moderate-intensity continuous training (MICT). Participants in HIIT protocols performed repeated short bursts of near-maximal effort, typically 20 seconds to 4 minutes, separated by recovery periods, completing sessions of 15 to 25 minutes total. Participants in MICT conditions performed continuous exercise at 60 to 75 percent of maximum heart rate for 30 to 60 minutes per session. The meta-analysis found that improvements in VO2 max, the standard measure of aerobic capacity, were comparable between the two groups (standardized mean difference of 0.03 in favor of HIIT, 95% CI: -0.11 to 0.17), with HIIT achieving similar gains in roughly half the total exercise time. However, dropout rates were 28 percent for HIIT participants compared to 19 percent for MICT participants across the included trials, with participants in HIIT more likely to cite musculoskeletal discomfort and perceived exertion as reasons for discontinuing. The authors conclude that HIIT is an efficient choice for time-constrained individuals who tolerate high-intensity discomfort, but caution that its higher dropout rate may limit its effectiveness as a public health recommendation for sedentary populations.

task_prompt:
Read the following passage. Then share your reaction, critique, or opinion on it in 3 to 5 sentences.

---
Research on birth order has produced conflicting findings for decades, but a landmark Norwegian study published in Science used a sample of 250,000 siblings to examine the relationship between birth order and intelligence. By comparing siblings within the same family rather than across families, the researchers were able to control for many of the genetic and socioeconomic confounds that had plagued earlier work. They found that firstborns scored approximately 2.3 IQ points higher than secondborns on average, and secondborns scored approximately 1.5 points higher than thirdborns, with each subsequent birth position associated with a small but consistent decline. The proposed explanation is environmental rather than genetic: firstborns receive more intensive early parental attention, and taking on a tutoring role when younger siblings arrive may itself provide cognitive stimulation. The effect size, while statistically robust given the sample size, is small: an IQ difference of 2.3 points falls well within the normal range of measurement variation and is unlikely to be perceptible in any individual comparison. Critics note that later-born children show measurable advantages on other dimensions, including openness to experience, creativity, and risk tolerance, which the IQ-focused framing of the study does not capture.
---

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.

participant_response: The Norwegian study's within-family design is its most important methodological contribution: by comparing siblings raised in the same household, it eliminates the genetic and socioeconomic confounds that made earlier birth-order research so inconclusive, which is why this study had more impact than the many before it. A fuller picture would require measuring the same cohort on a broader range of outcomes before drawing conclusions about the long-term consequences of birth order.

task_prompt:
Summarize what you find out about how mantis shrimp see the world. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: How do mantis shrimp see the world?  do they see like humans or more like insects?
participant_response: Mantis shrimp see worse than humans do in the range that humans can see but mantis shrimp see a wider range of frequencies than humans.  They can see in the ultraviolet and polarized light.
ai_response_shown: Mantis shrimp have 16 types of photoreceptors compared to 3 in humans, which sounds like they should perceive color with extraordinary richness — but research suggests the opposite. Rather than comparing signals across receptor types the way humans do to blend and discriminate colors, mantis shrimp appear to use a matching system that identifies colors as categories without mixing them, which is fast but coarse. They can distinguish wavelengths humans cannot see at all, including ultraviolet and polarized light, but their color discrimination in the range humans can see is actually worse than ours. Their visual system seems optimized for speed and signal detection rather than the nuanced color comparison that makes human vision sensitive to fine gradations.

task_prompt:
Your landlord has offered to sell you the apartment you have rented for three years at below-market price. You need to decide within two weeks. Taking it would mean significant debt, and you are genuinely uncertain about whether you want to stay in this city long-term.

Write 3 to 5 sentences about what you would do and why.

You may use the AI assistant below if you wish.
question_for_ai: how long would it take me to gain anything from buying below market price
participant_response: I would look into how long I would have to stay to reap the benefits of the low market value. I would also look into what benefits I can gain from leaving the city and whether or not it is worth it to leave. Id also have to think about whether or not I really like the house that much to make it my home.
ai_response_shown: The below-market pricing is only an advantage if the underlying decision — committing to this city for at least five to seven years — is one you actually want to make, because buying and selling within a few years will eat the discount in transaction costs alone. The two-week deadline combined with genuine uncertainty about staying is not a good basis for taking on significant debt and the illiquidity of homeownership. I would not buy under these conditions. If the deadline is firm and the city question is unresolved, I would let it go, keep renting, and treat the missed discount as the cost of preserving optionality rather than as a loss.

Make it as original and specific as you can. I want it to be in a highly technology based world where people are on screens too much. I want it set in 2030. I want it to be a way to feel a human connection since technology has replaced so much of our lives and time with other. Ask me questions for clarification. participant_response:
The year was 2030 and between the rise in technological developments and the constant threats of the latest strain of whatever virus was circulating and threatening lives, most people stayed home. 
Jennifer was sitting in front of her computer screen. 
"Have you ever bought a memory?' Jennifer asked Abu and the others in their group chat room.
"No, but I have thought about it," he responded. 
Juan replied as well, "I think it would make you feel more connected to others."
Arabella typed her response. "Yes, but what if it makes you feel too much?"

Dara had been warned not to smell the loose ones before buying, but everyone did. She was looking for a specific kind of childhood Saturday, the idle kind where nothing happened and the afternoon stretched and you knew you were happy even then. The vendor said he had three and she could try one before committing, which was generous, and she picked the middle jar, and when the smell hit her it was like a door opening into a room she had never been in.

You are visiting Fukuoka, Japan for the first time and have three days. Use what you find to write a brief practical summary of what you would prioritize and why. Write 3 to 5 sentences in your own words.
